text: The surgical field reveals numerous adhesions, with the stomach adhered to the anterior abdominal wall and liver. The surgeon carefully dissects and removes these adhesions to mobilize the retroesophageal space and the fundus of the stomach up to the spleen, without dividing the short gastric vessels.

The surgeon positions the fundus of the stomach behind the esophagus and applies two gastrogastric sutures, followed by a third suture connecting the esophagus to the stomach, securing the fundus in place as part of the Nissen fundoplication procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the duodenum, separating it from the pancreas by removing flimsy adhesions from a previous cholecystectomy. Using a blunt dissector, the surgeon enters the avascular field and divides the duodenum. The jejunum is then measured approximately 100 centimeters from the duodenojejunal flexure.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis between the loop of jejunum and the proximal duodenum using V-lock sutures, ensuring a secure connection with visible tension along the suture line.

The surgeon opens the duodenum and jejunum, applying a second layer of posterior wall sutures using V-lock sutures to ensure a watertight seal and flatten the posterior wall, preventing any ridge formation at the anastomosis site.

The surgeon continues anterior suturing to complete the anastomosis, ensuring a good connection. The efferent limb is measured to prevent reflux, and enterotomies are performed. The jejunum is anastomosed to the biliary limb using a trans-staple technique, followed by suturing to close the enterotomy. The mesenteric defect is addressed, and the biliary limb is disconnected from the duodenal-jejunal anastomosis.
